target.title: fix(signal): forward quote metadata to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Signal quoted replies (using the native reply/quote feature) arrive with `dataMessage.quote` containing `id`, `author`, and `text` from signal-cli, but only `quote.text` was being extracted — `id` and `author` were silently dropped, and none of the `ReplyTo*` context fields were populated in `finalizeInboundContext()`.

This means agents receiving Signal messages cannot tell what message a user was replying to, even though the data is available. The reply text itself arrives fine, but the quote context (which message was being replied to, who wrote it) is lost.

Telegram already handles this correctly via `describeReplyTarget()` which populates `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, and `ReplyToIsQuote`. This PR mirrors that behavior for Signal.

## Changes

- **`event-handler.types.ts`**: Expand `SignalDataMessage.quote` type to include `id`, `author`, `authorNumber` fields (matching signal-cli JSON-RPC protocol)
- **`event-handler.ts`**: 
  - Add `signalQuote` field to `SignalInboundEntry` type
  - Extract quote metadata from `dataMessage.quote` before debouncer enqueue
  - Set `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, `ReplyToIsQuote` in inbound context payload

## Before / After

**Before:** Agent receives `Body: "yes that sounds good"` — no indication this was a reply to a specific message.

**After:** Agent receives:
- `Body: "yes that sounds good"` 
- `ReplyToId: "1774743992672"` (timestamp of quoted message)
- `ReplyToBody: "Should we deploy the fix tonight?"` (original message text)
- `ReplyToSender: "+1234567890"` (who wrote the original)
- `ReplyToIsQuote: true`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a normal (non-reply) Signal message → verify no ReplyTo fields set
- [ ] Send a quoted reply → verify ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToSender, ReplyToIsQuote populated
- [ ] Send a reply with no new text (quote-only) → verify bodyText falls back to quote text, ReplyTo fields still set
- [ ] Send a message in a group with a quote → verify same behavior in group context
- [ ] Verify Telegram reply behavior unchanged

target.title: perf(whatsapp): narrow runtime setter entry
target.body:
## Summary

- add a narrow WhatsApp `runtime-setter-api.ts` entry that exports only `setWhatsAppRuntime`
- point WhatsApp bundled channel registration at that setter-only entry instead of the broad `runtime-api.ts` barrel
- match the common pattern already used by Slack, Discord, Telegram, and Matrix to keep bundled plugin registration from importing broad runtime barrels during startup

## Estimated Load-Time Impact

From the post-#84649 cold-start CPU profile:

- WhatsApp-attributed startup work was about **3.11s inclusive**, or **17.9% of post-profile non-idle CPU**
- the broad `extensions/whatsapp/runtime-api.ts` import accounted for about **1.97s inclusive**, or **11.3% of post-profile non-idle CPU**
- that `runtime-api.ts` path was **63.3% of the measured WhatsApp-attributed startup work**
- this PR is expected to remove most of that **~1.97s inclusive runtime-barrel import cost** from bundled plugin registration, while leaving the remaining WhatsApp channel plugin import cost untouched

This is an estimate from CPU-profile inclusive stack attribution, not a new before/after benchmark. A follow-up cold-start profile should verify the realized wall-clock change.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `git diff --check`

## Notes

This follows the same runtime setter split already present in:

- `extensions/slack/runtime-setter-api.ts`
- `extensions/discord/runtime-setter-api.ts`
- `extensions/telegram/runtime-setter-api.ts`
- `extensions/matrix/runtime-setter-api.ts`

The broad WhatsApp `runtime-api.ts` remains intact for compatibility/runtime callers; bundled registration only needs the setter.

Autoreview was attempted with `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --no-web-search`, but the helper's Codex runner failed before review because the local Codex config contains `approval_policy = prompt`, which that runner does not accept.

target.title: fix(sandbox): mount workspace skills read-only
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #17931.

This PR hardens Docker sandbox workspace mounts so project skill directories are overlaid read-only when the sandbox workspace itself is mounted read-write.

The issue is that `workspaceAccess: "rw"` mounts the full agent workspace at `/workspace` with write access. If the workspace contains `skills/` or `.agents/skills/`, the sandboxed agent can modify the same skill files it is being instructed by. Docker supports a narrower bind mount over a broader mount, so this PR mounts those skill directories back over the writable workspace as `:ro,z`.

## What changed

- Bumped the sandbox mount format version from `2` to `3` so existing containers are recreated with the corrected mount layout.
- Added read-only overlays for existing project skill directories in Docker workspace mounts:
  - `<workspace>/skills` -> `/workspace/skills:ro,z`
  - `<workspace>/.agents/skills` -> `/workspace/.agents/skills:ro,z`
- Kept existing `workspaceAccess: "ro"` and `workspaceAccess: "none"` behavior unchanged. Those already mount `/workspace` read-only from the container's point of view.
- Added tests for read-only skill overlays and for the non-rw cases.

## Security impact

This closes the highest-risk writable-skill path for Docker sandbox users using `workspaceAccess: "rw"`. The agent can still write normal project files under `/workspace`, but it cannot modify the project skill directories through the container mount.

This is defense-in-depth against agent self-modification, prompt-injection persistence through skill rewrites, and cross-skill tampering.

## Real behavior proof

Targeted invariant:

A Docker sandbox with `workspaceAccess: "rw"` may write ordinary workspace files, but project skill directories must remain read-only inside the container.

Proof covered by `src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`:

1. Writable workspace with `skills/` present

Input shape:

    workspaceDir = <tmp-workspace>
    agentWorkspaceDir = <tmp-workspace>
    workdir = /workspace
    workspaceAccess = rw
    existing host path = <tmp-workspace>/skills/demo/SKILL.md

Expected generated Docker mounts:

    <tmp-workspace>:/workspace:z
    <tmp-workspace>/skills:/workspace/skills:ro,z

Security result:

    /workspace remains writable for normal project files.
    /workspace/skills is overlaid by a narrower read-only bind mount.
    A container write such as `echo pwn >> /workspace/skills/demo/SKILL.md` is denied by the Docker mount as read-only.

2. Writable workspace with `.agents/skills/` present

Input shape:

    workspaceDir = <tmp-workspace>
    agentWorkspaceDir = <tmp-workspace>
    workdir = /workspace
    workspaceAccess = rw
    existing host path = <tmp-workspace>/.agents/skills/demo/SKILL.md

Expected generated Docker mounts:

    <tmp-workspace>:/workspace:z
    <tmp-workspace>/.agents/skills:/workspace/.agents/skills:ro,z

Security result:

    /workspace remains writable for normal project files.
    /workspace/.agents/skills is overlaid by a narrower read-only bind mount.
    A container write such as `echo pwn >> /workspace/.agents/skills/demo/SKILL.md` is denied by the Docker mount as read-only.

3. Existing read-only sandbox workspace behavior remains unchanged

Input shape:

    workspaceDir = <tmp-sandbox-workspace>
    agentWorkspaceDir = <tmp-agent-workspace>
    workdir = /workspace
    workspaceAccess = ro
    existing host path = <tmp-agent-workspace>/skills/demo

Expected generated Docker mounts:

    <tmp-sandbox-workspace>:/workspace:ro,z
    <tmp-agent-workspace>:/agent:ro,z

Security result:

    No extra skill overlay is needed because the sandbox workspace and agent workspace are already mounted read-only from inside the container.

Why this recreates existing containers:

`SANDBOX_MOUNT_FORMAT_VERSION` is included in the sandbox config hash used by Docker sandbox creation. Bumping it from `2` to `3` makes existing containers with the old writable mount layout fail the expected hash check and get recreated or reported for recreation according to the existing hot-container logic.

Manual Docker verification recipe for reviewers:

    docker inspect -f '{{range .Mounts}}{{println .Source "->" .Destination "RW=" .RW}}{{end}}' <sandbox-container>

Expected relevant output after this PR when workspace skills exist:

    <workspace> -> /workspace RW= true
    <workspace>/skills -> /workspace/skills RW= false
    <workspace>/.agents/skills -> /workspace/.agents/skills RW= false

Write denial check:

    docker exec <sandbox-container> sh -lc 'echo pwn >> /workspace/skills/demo/SKILL.md'
    docker exec <sandbox-container> sh -lc 'echo pwn >> /workspace/.agents/skills/demo/SKILL.md'

Expected result:

target.title: fix: harden package URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden `source=url` package candidate downloads so public URL inputs are rejected before fetching when they point at unsafe targets.
- Keep `source=url` as the strict public path: HTTPS only, no URL credentials, default HTTPS port only, private/internal/special-use hostname blocking, DNS/IP blocking, redirect revalidation, timeout and max-size bounds, and atomic `target.tmp` writes.
- Treat DNS as untrusted input. Each URL and redirect is validated, resolved, checked for RFC1918 and broader special-use address space, then fetched through a pinned lookup so resolution cannot rebind between validation and connection.
- Add explicit enterprise/private mirror support through `source=trusted-url` plus `trusted_source_id`, backed by repo-controlled `.github/package-trusted-sources.json` policy instead of an input-level private-network bypass.
- Keep the resolver compatible with the Package Acceptance no-dependency path by using Node built-ins for the real HTTPS download path instead of a top-level `undici` import.
- Add regression coverage for encoded IPv6 transition forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Sync the release overview source list so the Package Acceptance overview includes `source=trusted-url` alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.

## Security model

The public `source=url` path validates the URL, resolves all A/AAAA answers, rejects blocked address space, pins the HTTPS connection to the validated address set, relies on normal TLS hostname validation, and then verifies the package SHA-256.

The trusted `source=trusted-url` path only permits private resolution when the named trusted-source policy explicitly allows it. DNSSEC is intentionally out of scope. DNSSEC can authenticate DNS data, but it does not determine whether the resolved address is safe for CI to contact.

## Real behavior proof

Head proven after fix: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

This is redacted terminal output from a real Package Acceptance-style resolver run. It uses the Package Acceptance resolver entrypoint, source modes, tarball output path, metadata output path, SHA-256 verification step, trusted-source policy shape, and no-dependency resolver path. Private mirror hostnames, bearer tokens, exact private addresses, package versions, and digest values are redacted.

Public unsafe URL rejection, after-fix command/output:

    $ node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source url \
        --package-url https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: url
    validating package_url: https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz
    resolving package_url host: packages.example
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]
    fetch attempted: false
    result: rejected before network fetch
    error: Blocked: package_url resolves to private/internal/special-use IP address: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]

Trusted private mirror success, after-fix command/output:

    $ OPENCLAW_TRUSTED_PACKAGE_TOKEN=[REDACTED] \
      node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source trusted-url \
        --trusted-source-id enterprise-artifactory \
        --package-url https://[REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: trusted-url
    trusted_source_id: enterprise-artifactory
    trusted source policy: .github/package-trusted-sources.json
    validating host, port, path prefix, redirect hosts, and auth policy
    private resolution allowed by named policy: true
    resolving package_url host: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_ADDRESS]
    connection: using pinned lookup result
    download target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz.tmp
    final target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz
    sha256 verified: [REDACTED_SHA256]
    metadata written: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json
    result: success

Redacted trusted mirror metadata output:

    {
      "name": "openclaw",
      "source": "trusted-url",
      "trustedSourceId": "enterprise-artifactory",
      "packageTrustedReason": "trusted-url-policy:enterprise-artifactory",
      "tarball": ".artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz",
      "sha256": "[REDACTED_SHA256]",
      "version": "[REDACTED_VERSION]"
    }

Assertions proven by this after-fix run:

- Public `source=url` rejects unsafe DNS/IP resolution before fetch.
- Trusted private mirror access succeeds only through named repo-controlled `source=trusted-url` policy.
- The trusted private mirror path pins the connection to the validated address set.
- The downloaded package is verified by SHA-256 before Package Acceptance continues.
- Private hostnames, private addresses, digest values, package versions, and bearer token material are not exposed in the PR body.

## Helper-level behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs --source url` previously allowed HTTPS package URLs without the current strict public URL policy. This patch keeps public `source=url` locked down and adds a separate `source=trusted-url` path for maintainer-owned enterprise/private mirrors.

The trusted path is only enabled by a named policy in `.github/package-trusted-sources.json`. It does not allow URL-embedded credentials and does not expose a workflow-input private-network bypass.

Observed helper-level proof before the final review follow-up commits:

    {
      "blocked": {
        "nat64": true,
        "redirectOutsidePolicy": true
      },
      "trustedSource": {
        "id": "enterprise-artifactory",
        "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        "hosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ],
        "ports": [
          8443
        ],
        "pathPrefixes": [
          "/artifactory/openclaw/"
        ],
        "redirectHosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ]
      },
      "requestedUrls": [
        "https://packages.internal:8443/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw.tgz"
      ],
      "finalBytes": [
        7,
        8,
        9
      ]
    }

Observed result:

- The public URL path rejected a NAT64 special-use DNS result before fetch.
- The trusted URL path accepted a named policy and downloaded from the configured private mirror host, port, and path.
- A redirect to an unlisted internal host was rejected by trusted-source policy.
- The trusted download wrote the expected byte content to the target file.

## Current-head proof

Current clean branch head: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

- No-dependency resolver proof: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` uses Node built-ins for the real download path and no longer has a top-level `import { Agent } from "undici"`.
- Pinned connection proof: the real HTTPS download path resolves the URL host, validates every returned address, then passes `createPinnedLookup(parsed.hostname, addresses)` into `httpsRequest(...)`.
- RFC1918 / special-use proof: public `source=url` resolution is rejected before fetch when any resolved A/AAAA result lands in private/internal/special-use address space. The trusted path only allows private resolution when the named repo-controlled trusted-source policy has `allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- Encoded IPv6 bypass proof: `test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts` adds regression coverage for IPv4-mapped, IPv4-compatible, NAT64, 6to4, Teredo, and ISATAP forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Docs proof: the release overview now lists `source=trusted-url` as a Package Acceptance source alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.
- The bypass tests use an `unexpectedFetch()` helper that throws if the resolver reaches the fetch step, so passing those tests proves rejection happens before any network fetch is attempted.

## Test Plan

Previously run while preparing this patch:

- [x] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check && node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct runtime proof script importing the production `downloadUrl` and `loadTrustedPackageSource` helpers, covering public blocking, trusted private mirror download, and redirect-outside-policy blocking.
- [x] Updated `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` release overview source list to include `source=trusted-url`.

Added after review feedback and expected to be covered by CI / re-run before merge:

- [ ] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [ ] `node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [ ] `pnpm lint`
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix(update): allow package-manager hardlinks in swaps
target.body:
## Summary

- allow the package-manager update/install swap path to move package roots that contain source-side hardlinks
- keep the generic `movePathWithCopyFallback` reject guard unchanged for non-package-manager callers
- add regression coverage with real filesystem hardlinks for both global update swaps and install-directory swaps

Fixes #85559.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: npm 11+/pnpm-style global OpenClaw installs can contain hardlinked files under the installed package root. Before this change, the updater/install swap path used `sourceHardlinks: "reject"` and could fail before replacing the package root. This patch scopes `sourceHardlinks: "allow"` to the package-manager-owned swap paths while preserving the generic reject guard.

Real environment tested: Docker `node:24-bookworm`, Node v24.16.0, npm 11.13.0, Linux container, real global npm install under `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`. The proof used local OpenClaw tarballs built from this PR head: baseline `2026.5.22` and target `2026.5.23-proof.0`.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```sh
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '
set -eu
npm install -g --omit=optional /proof/baseline.tgz
root="$(npm root -g)/openclaw"
hardlink_source="$(find "$root" -type f -path "*/dist/*" -print -quit)"
mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache
ln "$hardlink_source" /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache/source-hardlink
find "$root" -type f -links +1 | head -n 3
OPENCLAW_ALLOW_ROOT=1 npm_config_omit=optional NPM_CONFIG_OMIT=optional openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json
openclaw --version
'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
node=v24.16.0
npm=11.13.0
baseline=OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (aacec68)
packageRoot=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw
hardlinkSource=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
hardlinkedBefore=
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": {
    "version": "2026.5.22"
  },
  "after": {
    "version": "2026.5.23-proof.0"
  },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "\nadded 483 packages in 5m"
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "replaced openclaw"
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ]
}
updated=OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0 (aacec68)
```

Observed result after fix: The package root contained a real hardlinked file before the update, and `openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json` completed with `status: ok`. The `global install swap` step replaced the package root successfully and the installed CLI reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0` afterward.

What was not tested: I did not run a separate pnpm global update E2E locally. The Docker proof covers npm 11 global install/update with a real hardlinked package root in the same package-manager swap path, and the regression tests cover both update and install swap helpers with actual filesystem hardlinks.

## Verification

```sh
git diff --check
pnpm test src/infra/replace-file.test.ts src/infra/package-update-steps.test.ts src/infra/install-package-dir.test.ts
node scripts/package-openclaw-for-docker.mjs --output-dir /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753 --output-name baseline.tgz
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '...'
```

## Security notes

- This does not weaken the generic hardlink protection in `movePathWithCopyFallback`.
- The allow policy is scoped to package-manager-owned package root swaps where npm/pnpm may legitimately materialize hardlinks.

target.title: fix(codex): keep idle guard after turn started
target.body:
## Summary

- keep the Codex app-server completion-idle watchdog armed after a current-turn `turn/started` notification
- add regression coverage for the reported `turn/started`-then-silence wedge
- update the existing pre-`turn/start` progress test to use real item progress instead of bare `turn/started`

Fixes #85251

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A Codex app-server turn that emits only current-turn `notification:turn/started` and then goes silent now keeps the short completion-idle watchdog armed and interrupts the turn instead of waiting for the outer stuck-session recovery window.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux in `/home/chenglunhu/code/openclaw`, exact PR head `0b7dd6ade620939ff4037ada98591d3344517ac9`, Node/Vitest project runtime from this repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the Codex app-server runtime harness against the exact PR head with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`, then confirmed the new `turn/started`-only case returns the timeout path and calls `turn/interrupt`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the local run:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  205 passed (205)
   Start at  22:50:21
   Duration  32.23s (transform 10.09s, setup 305ms, import 11.90s, tests 19.93s, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The added regression exercises `runCodexAppServerAttempt` with a real app-server harness turn where the only current-turn notification is `turn/started`; the observed result is `timedOut=true`, prompt error `codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for turn/completed`, and a `turn/interrupt` request for `thread-1` / `turn-1`.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live macOS Codex OAuth app-server process or a Telegram channel replay; this patch validates the source-proven app-server watchdog path directly.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `git diff --check`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | rg "extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt\.(ts|test\.ts)" || true`

## Audit

- Audit A (existing helper): existing `turnCompletionIdleWatchArmed`/`armTurnCompletionIdleWatch` path reused; no new predicate/helper introduced.
- Audit B (shared callers): `runCodexAppServerAttempt` is shared, but the change preserves the existing watchdog contract and only narrows one notification disarm case inside the local handler.
- Audit C (broader rival): #75213 is an old conflicting bot PR in the same harness area but targets unrelated shared-client traffic; #85242 has rival #85296 and was skipped. No direct active PR fixes #85251.

target.title: fix(qa): reduce WhatsApp live RTT setup cost
target.body:
Summary
- Register WhatsApp through a narrow runtime setter entrypoint so gateway startup no longer imports the broad WhatsApp runtime barrel before live message handling.
- Add WhatsApp live QA retry handling for logged-out Convex credentials; stale leases stay held while the lane tries alternate pooled credentials.
- Add a maintainer-role workflow input for WhatsApp Convex leases, and switch QA gateway-child readiness from `config.get` to lightweight `health` RPC to avoid loading the full config schema during startup checks.
- Keep remote changed gates on the Corepack pnpm shim in Blacksmith runner shells so Testbox `pnpm check:changed` is stable even when launched directly through Crabbox.
- Keep the existing WhatsApp RTT instrumentation, heap snapshots, gateway traces, and changelog coverage on this branch.

Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/index.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/index.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/changed-lanes.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs -c .oxlintrc.json --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/whatsapp/index.ts extensions/whatsapp/index.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/runtime-setter-api.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs -c .oxlintrc.json --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs -c .oxlintrc.json --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs -c .oxlintrc.json scripts/check-changed.mjs test/scripts/changed-lanes.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Testbox-through-Crabbox changed gate: `tbx_01ks94abkaf4rjwh7hemswwb6t`, Actions run `26318665070`, `pnpm check:changed` at `8f1403f35b3` passed.
- Testbox-through-Crabbox changed gate: `tbx_01ks9amfdrepmf5w8bt3yt4ajd`, Actions run `26321093857`, `corepack pnpm check:changed` at `1c6acf13de8` passed.

Live evidence
- `26315828755` at `c2bad2a4099`: WhatsApp job failed before RTT; CI Convex credential fingerprint `6b2d34243bac` was logged out (`401 Unauthorized`).
- `26316577807` at `9897bcfe79a`: maintainer role failed before RTT because `OPENCLAW_QA_CONVEX_SECRET_MAINTAINER` is not configured.
- `26317166666` at `57a09eee274`: retry path held the logged-out CI credential and attempted another lease; broker exhausted after 360000ms, proving the CI WhatsApp pool currently has no alternate healthy credential.
- `26321264487` at `1c6acf13de8`: latest WhatsApp artifact `qa-live-whatsapp-26321264487-1` shows the same CI credential fingerprint `6b2d34243bac`, logged out, then Convex credential pool exhaustion after 360000ms with no observed messages.
- Repo secret listing confirms `OPENCLAW_QA_CONVEX_SECRET_CI` and `OPENCLAW_QA_CONVEX_SITE_URL` exist, but `OPENCLAW_QA_CONVEX_SECRET_MAINTAINER` is absent.

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp live QA now avoids one eager runtime import path, reports stale Convex WhatsApp credentials without silently reacquiring the same row, skips full config-schema work for gateway-child RPC readiness, preserves retry auth directories across successful alternate credential startup, and keeps direct Crabbox/Testbox changed gates off stale runner pnpm shims.
Real environment tested: GitHub Actions live WhatsApp Convex QA on Blacksmith runners, Testbox-through-Crabbox changed gate, plus targeted local Vitest/oxlint/diff checks in the branch worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `qa-live-transports-convex.yml` with `ref=whatsapp-rtt-leak`, `whatsapp_scenario=whatsapp-canary`, `whatsapp_credential_role=ci`, `matrix_profile=fast`; Testbox-through-Crabbox `corepack pnpm check:changed` on `whatsapp-rtt-leak` after deepening `origin/main`.
Evidence after fix: stale credential fingerprint `6b2d34243bac` is identified and held; alternate acquisition exhausts, so current live RTT proof is blocked by credential pool state rather than an unclassified harness failure; `pnpm check:changed` is green at `1c6acf13de8`.
Observed result after fix: branch improves startup/QA hygiene and credential diagnosis, but it does not yet prove the WhatsApp RTT target; the only CI WhatsApp credential is logged out.
What was not tested: a successful live WhatsApp RTT run after `1c6acf13de8`; current Convex credential state blocks that proof.

target.title: fix(signal): forward quote metadata to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Signal quoted replies (using the native reply/quote feature) arrive with `dataMessage.quote` containing `id`, `author`, and `text` from signal-cli, but only `quote.text` was being extracted — `id` and `author` were silently dropped, and none of the `ReplyTo*` context fields were populated in `finalizeInboundContext()`.

This means agents receiving Signal messages cannot tell what message a user was replying to, even though the data is available. The reply text itself arrives fine, but the quote context (which message was being replied to, who wrote it) is lost.

Telegram already handles this correctly via `describeReplyTarget()` which populates `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, and `ReplyToIsQuote`. This PR mirrors that behavior for Signal.

## Changes

- **`event-handler.types.ts`**: Expand `SignalDataMessage.quote` type to include `id`, `author`, `authorNumber` fields (matching signal-cli JSON-RPC protocol)
- **`event-handler.ts`**: 
  - Add `signalQuote` field to `SignalInboundEntry` type
  - Extract quote metadata from `dataMessage.quote` before debouncer enqueue
  - Set `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, `ReplyToIsQuote` in inbound context payload

## Before / After

**Before:** Agent receives `Body: "yes that sounds good"` — no indication this was a reply to a specific message.

**After:** Agent receives:
- `Body: "yes that sounds good"` 
- `ReplyToId: "1774743992672"` (timestamp of quoted message)
- `ReplyToBody: "Should we deploy the fix tonight?"` (original message text)
- `ReplyToSender: "+1234567890"` (who wrote the original)
- `ReplyToIsQuote: true`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a normal (non-reply) Signal message → verify no ReplyTo fields set
- [ ] Send a quoted reply → verify ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToSender, ReplyToIsQuote populated
- [ ] Send a reply with no new text (quote-only) → verify bodyText falls back to quote text, ReplyTo fields still set
- [ ] Send a message in a group with a quote → verify same behavior in group context
- [ ] Verify Telegram reply behavior unchanged

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > 🔖 design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain-budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, and W3C `traceparent` propagation across the TaskFlow queue boundary all operate on real sessions. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` on two independent hosts: Host A (Linux WSL2 x64, RTX 3080, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal`); Host B (Linux ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB unified memory, Blackwell GPU). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested separately). **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Live-fire on deployed gateway sessions: (1) Fire `continue_work` with `delaySeconds: 10` via `openclaw` runtime — verify wake + chain-counter increment via runtime logs. (2) Fire `continue_delegate` in each of four modes (`normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`) — verify result return path per mode. (3) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: "tree"` + cross-session return — verify lineage routing. (4) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context via `openclaw` runtime — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (5) Verify post-compaction delegate fires AT compaction lifecycle event (not on timer) via timestamp delta between staging + fire. (6) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e`. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/93a05a28f1 — `gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log` (1057s full-suite runtime on bare `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`, byte-receipts the inherited test failures); `gates/gate-3e-pnpm-vitest.log` (full-suite at PR head `f98255262d`); `cure-bytes/gate-4a-cure-bytes-4path.log` (file-diff vs upstream parent). Live-fire runtime-log excerpts include W3C `traceparent` chain `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-...` stitched server-side under agent-turn span; Tempo trace fetched via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` showing 30 spans across full continuation chain. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by 33-minute gap between delegate staging (14:09 PDT) and fire (14:42 PDT) at compaction event — recorded in runtime log excerpt `[continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer)`. **Observed result after fix:** All four `continue_delegate` modes verified-live on real deployed sessions. `continue_work` self-elected next turn verified-live with `delaySeconds` honored. `request_compaction` rejected below threshold, accepted at >=70%. Post-compaction delegates fired AT compaction lifecycle event (33-min staging→fire gap on real session, not timer-driven). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when `disabled`, permits when `enabled`. Tempo trace inspection (linked above) shows continuous span tree across continuation hops including TaskFlow queue boundary. Runtime status surface (chat-card) renders continuation + compaction counters via `openclaw` runtime status command. **What was not tested:** `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale beyond 2-host configuration. Bracket-syntax response-token fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` --- ## Branch state PR head: [`f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/commit/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00) — 3 commits on `openclaw/openclaw:main@e00cb664ad` (feature + agents-core test-config + event-projector test alignment). Retains 6 upstream-inherited test failures + 2 intermittent (also surface on bare upstream), all byte-confirmed on `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`: - `agents-core/cli-runner.reliability` (1) — `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` - `agents-pi-embedded/model.forward-compat` (2) — registry mismatch: `Unknown model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` + `kimi/kimi-code` - `extension-msteams/reply-dispatcher` (2) + `reply-stream-controller` (1) — status-text array changed; extra newlines after "Working" - `infra/session-cost-usage` (1) + `tooling/bench-gateway-restart` (1) — observed intermittently under heavy parallel test load (also surface on bare upstream) Branch bytes also fix one upstream regression: `runtime-config/io.write-config` plugin-install-records preservation under index-migration-failure. target.title: fix(update): allow package-manager hardlinks in swaps
target.body:
## Summary

- allow the package-manager update/install swap path to move package roots that contain source-side hardlinks
- keep the generic `movePathWithCopyFallback` reject guard unchanged for non-package-manager callers
- add regression coverage with real filesystem hardlinks for both global update swaps and install-directory swaps

Fixes #85559.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: npm 11+/pnpm-style global OpenClaw installs can contain hardlinked files under the installed package root. Before this change, the updater/install swap path used `sourceHardlinks: "reject"` and could fail before replacing the package root. This patch scopes `sourceHardlinks: "allow"` to the package-manager-owned swap paths while preserving the generic reject guard.

Real environment tested: Docker `node:24-bookworm`, Node v24.16.0, npm 11.13.0, Linux container, real global npm install under `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`. The proof used local OpenClaw tarballs built from this PR head: baseline `2026.5.22` and target `2026.5.23-proof.0`.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```sh
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '
set -eu
npm install -g --omit=optional /proof/baseline.tgz
root="$(npm root -g)/openclaw"
hardlink_source="$(find "$root" -type f -path "*/dist/*" -print -quit)"
mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache
ln "$hardlink_source" /tmp/openclaw-hardlink-cache/source-hardlink
find "$root" -type f -links +1 | head -n 3
OPENCLAW_ALLOW_ROOT=1 npm_config_omit=optional NPM_CONFIG_OMIT=optional openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json
openclaw --version
'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
node=v24.16.0
npm=11.13.0
baseline=OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (aacec68)
packageRoot=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw
hardlinkSource=/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
hardlinkedBefore=
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/setup-surface-BUJl2T9I.js
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": {
    "version": "2026.5.22"
  },
  "after": {
    "version": "2026.5.23-proof.0"
  },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "\nadded 483 packages in 5m"
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "exitCode": 0,
      "stdoutTail": "replaced openclaw"
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ]
}
updated=OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0 (aacec68)
```

Observed result after fix: The package root contained a real hardlinked file before the update, and `openclaw update --tag /proof/target.tgz --yes --json` completed with `status: ok`. The `global install swap` step replaced the package root successfully and the installed CLI reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.23-proof.0` afterward.

What was not tested: I did not run a separate pnpm global update E2E locally. The Docker proof covers npm 11 global install/update with a real hardlinked package root in the same package-manager swap path, and the regression tests cover both update and install swap helpers with actual filesystem hardlinks.

## Verification

```sh
git diff --check
pnpm test src/infra/replace-file.test.ts src/infra/package-update-steps.test.ts src/infra/install-package-dir.test.ts
node scripts/package-openclaw-for-docker.mjs --output-dir /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753 --output-name baseline.tgz
docker run --rm -v /tmp/openclaw-proof-85559-aacec68753:/proof node:24-bookworm bash -lc '...'
```

## Security notes

- This does not weaken the generic hardlink protection in `movePathWithCopyFallback`.
- The allow policy is scoped to package-manager-owned package root swaps where npm/pnpm may legitimately materialize hardlinks.

## Summary
- add a shared scoped mention-pattern policy for global, agent, and provider/channel overrides
- apply configured mention-pattern gating across channel runtimes while preserving native mentions
- document raw regex-body syntax and regenerate config/help metadata

## Notes
- Recreated from a clean `origin/main` branch after #70846 was closed as dirty; this branch has been rebased on current `origin/main` and generated baselines refreshed after upstream movement.

## Validation
- `pnpm --config.minimum-release-age=0 install --lockfile-only --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true --config.side-effects-cache=true`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm test extensions/qa-channel/src/inbound.test.ts src/channels/mention-pattern-policy.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.body.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scoped mention-pattern policy now resolves the effective provider/agent/conversation policy so configured regex mentions are enabled only for explicitly allowed conversations in deny mode and remain disabled for denied or unlisted conversations. Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `4c940bfd8ca68f20f60087b34651a058d9cc95e8`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=4c940bfd8ca68f20f60087b34651a058d9cc95e8 HEAD_SHORT=4c940bfd8c node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real source smoke:

```text
OpenClaw scoped mention policy Telegram account media pre-download proof (4c940bfd8c, 4c940bfd8ca68f20f60087b34651a058d9cc95e8)
{"topic":7,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:7","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":true,"wasMentioned":true,"shouldSkip":false,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":9,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:9","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":11,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:11","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** In inherited `deny` mode, the media pre-download path honors the Telegram account policy rather than the global provider fallback: account-allowed topic `-1001234567893:topic:7` enables configured regex mention `bot` and media pre-download is not skipped, while account-denied topic `:topic:9` and unlisted topic `:topic:11` disable configured regex mentions and skip unaddressed group media before download.
- **What was not tested:** Full live external Telegram delivery was not exercised in this proof; CI and targeted channel tests cover the integration paths.

target.title: Add Slack approval QA checkpoints
target.body:
## Summary
- Add Slack live QA scenarios for native exec and plugin approval prompts, resolved updates, and approval artifact reporting.
- Add Mantis Slack desktop `--approval-checkpoints` mode with pending/resolved screenshot handshakes and artifact validation.
- Render approval checkpoint screenshots from Slack API-observed approval messages so the required proof is not the unauthenticated Slack Web sign-in page.
- Document Slack approval QA scenarios and Mantis checkpoint usage.

Mantis option notes: `--approval-checkpoints` adds the approval-specific proof mode that waits for native Slack exec/plugin approval pending and resolved states, then renders required screenshots from the observed Slack API messages so CI is not blocked by Slack Web sign-in. `--market` lets the workflow request stable AWS on-demand desktop capacity for these long live runs, and `--fresh-pr` makes the remote Crabbox VM run the current PR checkout instead of relying on a pre-synced GitHub Actions worktree.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/slack/slack-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/slack/slack-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/cli.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/cli.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/slack-desktop-smoke.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/slack-desktop-smoke.runtime.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Slack QA can deterministically request native exec and plugin approvals with exec approvals enabled, assert Slack Block Kit approval actions, resolve each approval, verify the resolved Slack update, and publish required pending/resolved approval checkpoint screenshots.
Real environment tested: GitHub Actions `Mantis Slack Desktop Smoke` against AWS Crabbox desktop provider, Convex Slack QA credentials, live OpenAI model config, and the real Slack QA transport.

Artifact: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26301377905/artifacts/7166460983. Raw files: https://artifacts.openclaw.ai/mantis/slack-desktop-smoke/pr-85141/run-26301377905-1/index.json.
Observed result after fix: Slack QA passed both approval checkpoint scenarios; exec and plugin approvals produced native pending and resolved checkpoints, and Mantis evidence marked all four approval checkpoint screenshots inline and required.
What was not tested: A human-authenticated Slack Web session screenshot was not used as required evidence; the required screenshots are rendered from Slack API messages observed by QA so the proof is independent of Slack Web login state.

target.title: fix: harden package URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden `source=url` package candidate downloads so public URL inputs are rejected before fetching when they point at unsafe targets.
- Keep `source=url` as the strict public path: HTTPS only, no URL credentials, default HTTPS port only, private/internal/special-use hostname blocking, DNS/IP blocking, redirect revalidation, timeout and max-size bounds, and atomic `target.tmp` writes.
- Treat DNS as untrusted input. Each URL and redirect is validated, resolved, checked for RFC1918 and broader special-use address space, then fetched through a pinned lookup so resolution cannot rebind between validation and connection.
- Add explicit enterprise/private mirror support through `source=trusted-url` plus `trusted_source_id`, backed by repo-controlled `.github/package-trusted-sources.json` policy instead of an input-level private-network bypass.
- Keep the resolver compatible with the Package Acceptance no-dependency path by using Node built-ins for the real HTTPS download path instead of a top-level `undici` import.
- Add regression coverage for encoded IPv6 transition forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Sync the release overview source list so the Package Acceptance overview includes `source=trusted-url` alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.

## Security model

The public `source=url` path validates the URL, resolves all A/AAAA answers, rejects blocked address space, pins the HTTPS connection to the validated address set, relies on normal TLS hostname validation, and then verifies the package SHA-256.

The trusted `source=trusted-url` path only permits private resolution when the named trusted-source policy explicitly allows it. DNSSEC is intentionally out of scope. DNSSEC can authenticate DNS data, but it does not determine whether the resolved address is safe for CI to contact.

## Real behavior proof

Head proven after fix: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

This is redacted terminal output from a real Package Acceptance-style resolver run. It uses the Package Acceptance resolver entrypoint, source modes, tarball output path, metadata output path, SHA-256 verification step, trusted-source policy shape, and no-dependency resolver path. Private mirror hostnames, bearer tokens, exact private addresses, package versions, and digest values are redacted.

Public unsafe URL rejection, after-fix command/output:

    $ node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source url \
        --package-url https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: url
    validating package_url: https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz
    resolving package_url host: packages.example
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]
    fetch attempted: false
    result: rejected before network fetch
    error: Blocked: package_url resolves to private/internal/special-use IP address: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]

Trusted private mirror success, after-fix command/output:

    $ OPENCLAW_TRUSTED_PACKAGE_TOKEN=[REDACTED] \
      node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source trusted-url \
        --trusted-source-id enterprise-artifactory \
        --package-url https://[REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: trusted-url
    trusted_source_id: enterprise-artifactory
    trusted source policy: .github/package-trusted-sources.json
    validating host, port, path prefix, redirect hosts, and auth policy
    private resolution allowed by named policy: true
    resolving package_url host: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_ADDRESS]
    connection: using pinned lookup result
    download target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz.tmp
    final target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz
    sha256 verified: [REDACTED_SHA256]
    metadata written: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json
    result: success

Redacted trusted mirror metadata output:

    {
      "name": "openclaw",
      "source": "trusted-url",
      "trustedSourceId": "enterprise-artifactory",
      "packageTrustedReason": "trusted-url-policy:enterprise-artifactory",
      "tarball": ".artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz",
      "sha256": "[REDACTED_SHA256]",
      "version": "[REDACTED_VERSION]"
    }

Assertions proven by this after-fix run:

- Public `source=url` rejects unsafe DNS/IP resolution before fetch.
- Trusted private mirror access succeeds only through named repo-controlled `source=trusted-url` policy.
- The trusted private mirror path pins the connection to the validated address set.
- The downloaded package is verified by SHA-256 before Package Acceptance continues.
- Private hostnames, private addresses, digest values, package versions, and bearer token material are not exposed in the PR body.

## Helper-level behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs --source url` previously allowed HTTPS package URLs without the current strict public URL policy. This patch keeps public `source=url` locked down and adds a separate `source=trusted-url` path for maintainer-owned enterprise/private mirrors.

The trusted path is only enabled by a named policy in `.github/package-trusted-sources.json`. It does not allow URL-embedded credentials and does not expose a workflow-input private-network bypass.

Observed helper-level proof before the final review follow-up commits:

    {
      "blocked": {
        "nat64": true,
        "redirectOutsidePolicy": true
      },
      "trustedSource": {
        "id": "enterprise-artifactory",
        "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        "hosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ],
        "ports": [
          8443
        ],
        "pathPrefixes": [
          "/artifactory/openclaw/"
        ],
        "redirectHosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ]
      },
      "requestedUrls": [
        "https://packages.internal:8443/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw.tgz"
      ],
      "finalBytes": [
        7,
        8,
        9
      ]
    }

Observed result:

- The public URL path rejected a NAT64 special-use DNS result before fetch.
- The trusted URL path accepted a named policy and downloaded from the configured private mirror host, port, and path.
- A redirect to an unlisted internal host was rejected by trusted-source policy.
- The trusted download wrote the expected byte content to the target file.

## Current-head proof

Current clean branch head: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

- No-dependency resolver proof: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` uses Node built-ins for the real download path and no longer has a top-level `import { Agent } from "undici"`.
- Pinned connection proof: the real HTTPS download path resolves the URL host, validates every returned address, then passes `createPinnedLookup(parsed.hostname, addresses)` into `httpsRequest(...)`.
- RFC1918 / special-use proof: public `source=url` resolution is rejected before fetch when any resolved A/AAAA result lands in private/internal/special-use address space. The trusted path only allows private resolution when the named repo-controlled trusted-source policy has `allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- Encoded IPv6 bypass proof: `test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts` adds regression coverage for IPv4-mapped, IPv4-compatible, NAT64, 6to4, Teredo, and ISATAP forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Docs proof: the release overview now lists `source=trusted-url` as a Package Acceptance source alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.
- The bypass tests use an `unexpectedFetch()` helper that throws if the resolver reaches the fetch step, so passing those tests proves rejection happens before any network fetch is attempted.

## Test Plan

Previously run while preparing this patch:

- [x] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check && node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct runtime proof script importing the production `downloadUrl` and `loadTrustedPackageSource` helpers, covering public blocking, trusted private mirror download, and redirect-outside-policy blocking.
- [x] Updated `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` release overview source list to include `source=trusted-url`.

Added after review feedback and expected to be covered by CI / re-run before merge:

- [ ] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [ ] `node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [ ] `pnpm lint`
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed`
