target.title: fix(agents): keep cli-auth-epoch stable when local credential file presence flips
target.body:
## Summary

- Extend `shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` at the prepare/backend auth-epoch policy seam to skip local credentials for identity-less OAuth profiles (no email, no accountId).
- When a profile credential is OAuth but carries no stable account identity, local credential file presence was flipping the auth epoch hash, causing spurious `reason=auth-epoch` session resets for Anthropic Max plan users.
- Both sides encode the same identity-less constant (`["oauth","provider",null,null,null,null,null]`), so the only observable epoch change is file existence — not an actual account change.
- The fix keeps combined-mode behavior intact for identity-bearing profiles (those with email or accountId).
- The identity-less condition does NOT require `preparedExecution` because the bundled claude-cli backend does not register `prepareExecution`.

### What did NOT change

- Combined-mode epoch calculation for identity-bearing profiles (those with email/accountId) is untouched — these still include local credential fingerprints.
- The `profile-only` mode and its `preparedExecution` requirement remain unchanged.
- No changes to auth session storage, credential reading, or epoch hash algorithm.
- `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump from 4 to 5 is the only version change; no migration or data format changes.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Agents/cli-auth-epoch
- [x] CLI/prepare
- [ ] Core messaging
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] Channel adapters
- [ ] Configuration

## Root Cause

`shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` in `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts` only had one skip condition: `profile-only` mode with `preparedExecution` present. Identity-less OAuth profiles (e.g. Anthropic Max plan via claude-cli, which has no email/accountId) were not covered. When the local CLI credential file appeared or disappeared (e.g. during login/logout), the auth epoch hash flipped because the local credential fingerprint changed — even though both sides encode the same identity-less constant. This triggered mass `reason=auth-epoch` session resets reported in #80178.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Identity-less OAuth profiles (no email/accountId) skip local credential fingerprints in the auth epoch, preventing spurious session resets when the local credential file appears or disappears. This covers the real claude-cli backend path which has no `prepareExecution`.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
    import { shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch } from './src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.js';
    const now = Date.now() + 60000;
    const cases = [
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now },
          preparedExecution: {} } },
      { label: 'identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:work',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'anthropic', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now,
            email: 'user@example.com' } } },
      { label: 'no auth profile selected',
        args: { authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'profile-only (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authEpochMode: 'profile-only', authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
    ];
    for (const c of cases) {
      console.log(c.label + ':', shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch(c.args));
    }
    "

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` invocation:

    claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution): true
    claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution): true
    identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution): false
    no auth profile selected: false
    profile-only (no preparedExecution): false

**Observed result after fix:** The `node --import tsx` terminal output confirms: the claude-cli identity-less path correctly returns `true` (skip) without requiring `preparedExecution`; identity-bearing profiles return `false` (combined mode); profile-only without `preparedExecution` returns `false`. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 ensures existing sessions recalculate epochs under the corrected policy.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end session reuse with live Claude CLI credentials. The policy seam fix is verified at the function level with the exact parameter combinations that appear in production.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts` — 22 passed
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-auth-epoch.test.ts` — 13 passed (includes new `keeps identity-less claude-cli OAuth epochs stable across local file presence (#80178)` test)
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-session.test.ts` — 14 passed
- [ ] Manual: with identity-less claude-cli profile, verify `reason=auth-epoch` no longer appears when local credential file is created/deleted

## User-visible Changes

Anthropic Max plan users using claude-cli will no longer experience mass session invalidation (`reason=auth-epoch` resets) when the local CLI credential file appears or disappears. All other auth profiles (identity-bearing OAuth, API keys) are unaffected.

## Diagram

    Before:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint flips on file presence change -> epoch reset

    After:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> identity-less OAuth (no email, no accountId)? skip  <-- new
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint excluded -> epoch stable

## Security Impact

No security boundary changes. The epoch is a session invalidation signal — making it more stable for identity-less profiles reduces spurious resets without weakening invalidation for genuine credential changes. Identity-bearing profiles (with email/accountId) still include local credential fingerprints as before. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 triggers a one-time recalculation but does not invalidate stored sessions.

Closes #80178

target.title: fix(ollama): bypass managed proxy for loopback embeddings
target.body:
## Problem

When `proxy.enabled=true`, OpenClaw routes strict HTTP fetches through the managed forward proxy. That is the right default for ordinary egress, but it breaks the bundled Ollama memory embedding provider when the configured Ollama `baseUrl` points at a host-local loopback service such as `http://127.0.0.1:11434`: a remote or policy-strict proxy cannot reach the OpenClaw host's loopback service.

PR #80751 made configured local provider origins trusted for SSRF policy purposes, but it did not change managed-proxy routing. PR #83255 addresses a separate browser/CDP loopback path. This PR covers the remaining Ollama memory embedding path.

## Change and value

This PR lets the Ollama memory embedding provider mark its exact configured loopback `baseUrl` as eligible for a guarded managed-proxy direct path. When the managed proxy is active, `fetchWithSsrFGuard` still resolves and validates the destination first, then uses a pinned direct dispatcher only when the request origin exactly matches the configured provider origin, the configured origin is loopback, and the resolved target addresses are loopback.

The practical effect is that host-local Ollama memory embeddings can work with `proxy.enabled=true` without creating a general local-network bypass. Public, LAN/private, and otherwise non-loopback Ollama origins stay on the managed proxy path.

## Who is affected, and who is not

Affected:

- Users running the bundled Ollama memory embedding provider against a loopback Ollama `baseUrl` while the managed proxy is active.
- Operators who need `proxy.loopbackMode` to keep explicit control over loopback behavior.

Not affected:

- Public Ollama Cloud or other public embedding endpoints: these stay on the managed proxy path.
- LAN/private non-loopback Ollama endpoints: these are not granted a direct proxy bypass by this PR.
- Browser/CDP loopback discovery and WebSocket routing: that is the separate #83255 scope.
- General user WebUIs, arbitrary local model servers, and other local-network targets: this does not add a broad bypass.
- Redirects away from the configured Ollama origin: those are re-evaluated and do not inherit the bypass.

## Why now

Issue #81249 still appears unresolved for users whose remaining failure is Ollama/local embeddings under the managed proxy. The existing fixes cover adjacent pieces, but not this transport decision. This PR is intentionally narrow so maintainers can review the Ollama embedding behavior separately from the browser/CDP change in #83255.

## Implementation

- Adds an internal `managedProxyBypass` marker to guarded fetch options for the specific `configured-local-origin` case.
- Keeps the bypass marker internal to core and bundled providers; this PR does not publish managed-proxy bypass types through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime`.
- In managed-proxy strict mode, resolves the request hostname under the existing SSRF policy before deciding transport.
- Uses direct pinned dispatch only when the request origin exactly matches the configured origin, the configured origin is loopback, all resolved addresses are loopback, and `proxy.loopbackMode` allows it.
- Keeps public, LAN/private, and otherwise non-loopback configured origins on the managed proxy path.
- Honors `proxy.loopbackMode: "proxy"` by leaving loopback traffic on the proxy path.
- Honors `proxy.loopbackMode: "block"` by rejecting loopback provider traffic before opening a connection, including bracketed IPv6 loopback URLs.
- Switches the Ollama embedding SSRF policy from configured hostname trust to configured exact-origin trust.
- Documents the narrower Ollama memory embedding behavior in the provider and network proxy docs.

## Risks and review notes

This intentionally treats an exact configured loopback Ollama embedding origin as operator-trusted local IPC for the bundled embedding provider. That matches the local-provider direction from #80751, but it is still a proxy policy boundary and should get maintainer review.

The managed-proxy bypass remains an internal core/bundled-provider contract, not a new public plugin SDK API.

This PR is still draft until the remaining remote full-suite red-path classification and review gates are complete.

## Out of scope

- It does not change the generic model/chat provider transport path.
- It does not address browser/CDP loopback behavior; see #83255.
- It does not install or run a real Ollama daemon.
- It does not introduce a new public config knob.

## Verification

Current exact-head remote validation after the SDK-boundary/docs follow-up (`us-east1-b`, `e2-standard-8`) from a fresh `openclaw/openclaw` clone at exact PR head `8af6838ed8` / `8af6838ed873bfc9a789230e911ccfcbbd3b4b2a`, Node v24.15.0:

- Clean-env leakage check: `(none)`.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- Proof harness with fake loopback Ollama `/api/embed` and fake managed proxy:
  - `direct exact-origin Ollama embedding bypass: ok`
  - `proxy.loopbackMode=proxy keeps Ollama embedding on proxy path: ok`
  - `redirect away from configured Ollama origin remains blocked: ok`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` (`1 file / 68 tests passed`)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` (`1 file / 20 tests passed`)
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`

Current changed-file scope is 6 files: Ollama provider docs, network proxy docs, the Ollama embedding provider and tests, and the fetch guard and SSRF tests. No package manifest or lockfile files are changed.

Prior broad remote validation before the SDK-boundary/docs follow-up, from exact PR head `0aa6bc355fae4268ab2b0bba6dfa66ee02be61a8`, also passed:

- Full extension providers/extensions Vitest shard (`208 files / 2755 tests passed`, including `extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.test.ts` with 20 tests passed)
- Full unit Vitest shard (`59 files / 320 tests passed`)
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint`
- `pnpm check:import-cycles`
- `pnpm build:strict-smoke`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm plugins:assets:build`

Full-suite status:

- Full `pnpm test` was run remotely at prior exact PR head `0aa6bc355fae4268ab2b0bba6dfa66ee02be61a8` and exited red.
- A same-environment full `pnpm test` comparison against `origin/main` at `4df34cb79023cb22ea69dd7c5787fef9fa1178d3` also exited red (`8` failed files / `31` failed tests parsed):
  - `src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-registry.announce-loop-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.acp-abort.test.ts`
  - `src/commands/models/list.list-command.forward-compat.test.ts`
- Several visible PR-head full-suite failures matched failures reproduced on the `origin/main` comparison, including the live model switch, PI embedded runner, bash-tools approval follow-up, and subagent announce/loop-guard areas.
- The current follow-up commit after `0aa6bc355f` only removes the public SDK re-export and tightens docs. It is covered at `8af6838ed8` by the exact-head proof harness, focused SSRF/Ollama tests, API check, and diff check above.
- Remaining draft blocker: the full suite is not green in this remote environment, and any red paths not proven inherited from `origin/main` still need final classification before this leaves draft.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Ollama memory embeddings configured to a host-local loopback `baseUrl` should still work when `OPENCLAW_PROXY_ACTIVE=1` and `HTTP(S)_PROXY` points at a managed proxy that cannot reach host loopback. The same path should keep `proxy.loopbackMode=proxy` on the proxy path and should not trust redirects away from the configured origin.

**Real environment tested:** Remote GCP Spot VM (`us-east1-b`, `e2-standard-8`), Node v24.15.0, exact PR head `8af6838ed873bfc9a789230e911ccfcbbd3b4b2a`. The proof starts a fake Ollama-compatible `/api/embed` service on `127.0.0.1:<random>` and a fake managed proxy on `127.0.0.1:<random>` that returns 503 for ordinary and CONNECT requests. It sets `OPENCLAW_PROXY_ACTIVE=1`, points `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` at the fake proxy, and clears `NO_PROXY`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** A remote proof harness imported `extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.ts`, created the real Ollama embedding provider with `remote.baseUrl` set to the fake loopback service, and executed three requests: default `gateway-only`, `proxy.loopbackMode=proxy`, and a cross-origin loopback redirect.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
direct exact-origin Ollama embedding bypass: ok
proxy.loopbackMode=proxy keeps Ollama embedding on proxy path: ok
[security] blocked URL fetch (memory-remote) targetOrigin=http://127.0.0.1:<redirect-port> reason=Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address
redirect away from configured Ollama origin remains blocked: ok
```

**Observed result after fix:** In default `gateway-only` mode, the real Ollama embedding provider code reached the fake host-local Ollama service directly, returned the normalized vector `[0.6, 0.8]`, and did not hit the fake proxy. With `proxy.loopbackMode=proxy`, the same request hit and failed through the fake proxy. A redirect from the configured origin to a different loopback port was blocked by the existing SSRF guard.

**What was not tested:** A real Ollama daemon/local model was not exercised. The proof covers the provider transport and response-handling path with an Ollama-compatible fake `/api/embed` endpoint and fake managed proxy, not actual local model execution.

Related #81249.

target.title: Add development trace UI
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a source-checkout-only development LLM trace recorder for full prompt/tool payload debugging.
- Exposes `traces.capabilities`, `traces.list`, and `traces.get` Gateway methods behind explicit development flags, admin scope, and a strict direct-loopback browser Control UI grant.
- Adds a Traces Control UI view with a compact trace selector plus readable prompt-message, tool-argument, parameter, response, and raw-payload sections, with mocked Gateway E2E coverage.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/llm-dev-tracing.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/traces.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/traces.e2e.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/styles/components.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check ui/src/ui/views/traces.ts ui/src/styles/components.css ui/src/ui/e2e/traces.e2e.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt 'Only review the tracing UI patch in ui/src/ui/views/traces.ts, ui/src/styles/components.css, and ui/src/ui/e2e/traces.e2e.test.ts. Treat unrelated untracked docs/refactor/*.md files as out of scope; they pre-existed this task and should not be reported.'`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Development-only tracing UI shows captured LLM prompt payloads and tool schemas while remaining disabled outside an explicit source-checkout loopback debugging session.

Real environment tested: Local Control UI Playwright/Chromium E2E server with mocked Gateway WebSocket responses, using the repo's Control UI E2E helper.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Captured a Playwright browser recording through the Traces route with mock trace capability enabled, including hidden reasoning/tool-call response deltas and visible answer deltas, then ran the committed trace E2E command above.

Evidence after fix: Current recording captured at local artifact `.artifacts/control-ui-e2e/tracing-ui-readable/page@c8ae1e5a24ef13f63ff45930fd196950.webm`; screenshot captured at `.artifacts/control-ui-e2e/tracing-ui-readable/tracing-ui-readable.png`; E2E asserts the compact trace row omits request-size metadata, prompt text is readable, tool arguments render as rows, visible response text renders, and hidden reasoning/tool-call deltas do not appear as answer text.

Observed result after fix: The Traces tab rendered the selected `openai/gpt-5.5` trace with a narrow left selector and a readable detail panel for system/user messages, tool parameters, invocation parameters, and final answer text, while still keeping raw message/schema/payload disclosures available.

What was not tested: A live provider request with real credentials; the proof intentionally uses deterministic mocked Gateway data to avoid recording real prompts or secrets.

target.title: fix(telegram): transient Telegram pairing prompts
target.body: ## Summary The behavior I'm seeing is transient Telegram pairing requests after an idle: After a deep dive this is most likely caused by FD pressure. But in that case it should error not send a confusing and potentially time-consuming pairing request ## Solution - Avoid treating transient Telegram pairing-store read failures as an empty pairing allowlist that triggers a new pairing challenge. - Skip pairing-store reads when the effective DM policy is not pairing, or when configured `allowFrom` already authorizes the sender. - Preserve per-DM pairing policy resolution when flushing long text fragments. ## Verification - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> 91 passed - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts` -> 5 passed - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> passed - `git diff --check` -> passed - `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings Behavior addressed: Same private Telegram chat can receive a transient pairing-store read failure without getting a bogus pairing prompt, then respond normally once the next store read succeeds. Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on Node/Vitest with mocked Telegram bot handlers and pairing-store dependency failures. Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the two focused Telegram test files above, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and autoreview. Evidence after fix: `bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` passes 91 tests, including the same-chat back-to-back message regression where the first store read rejects and the second returns the paired sender id. Observed result after fix: No pairing request is upserted on transient store failure; configured/open DMs avoid unnecessary store reads; per-DM pairing store authorization still works for text-fragment flushes. What was not tested: No live Telegram round trip was run; this is covered with mocked bot handler regressions rather than Crabbox/live Telegram proof.

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

target.title: fix(plugins): memoize loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync on hot path
target.body:
## Summary

- Memoize `loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync` result in `resolvePluginLoadCacheContext` so the sync file read does not fire on every `resolvePluginTools()` call, including LRU cache hits.
- Invalidate the memoized install records alongside the plugin loader cache in `clearPluginLoaderCache` and `clearPluginRegistryLoadCache`.

Install records do not change at runtime, so the synchronous file read was redundant after the first call. This was causing unnecessary I/O on every plugin tool resolution, compounding under concurrent embedded runs.

## Relationship to #85026

PR #85026 (merged) addresses the same root cause by threading `installRecords` through `PluginLoadOptions` from the upstream `PluginMetadataSnapshot`. That covers the primary hot paths where the snapshot is available.

This PR complements #85026 by memoizing the **fallback path**: when `options.installRecords` is not provided by upstream callers, `resolvePluginLoadCacheContext` still falls back to `loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync({ env })`. Without memoization, any code path that invokes `resolvePluginTools()` without passing install records will trigger a redundant synchronous file read on every call.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `resolvePluginLoadCacheContext` falls back to `loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync` (a synchronous file read) whenever `options.installRecords` is not provided by the caller. This fallback still fires on every `resolvePluginTools()` call, compounding under concurrent embedded runs (#77348). PR #85026 covers the main hot paths; this PR memoizes the remaining fallback.

**Real environment tested:** Node.js v22.22.0 on Linux (same runtime as OpenClaw production). Ran a benchmark script that simulates 100 consecutive fallback invocations with a 1.6 KB install records file (50 plugin entries), measuring file read count and elapsed time before and after the memoization patch.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```bash
node memoize_benchmark.mjs
```
The script creates a mock install records JSON file, then runs 100 iterations of the original (un-memoized) read and 100 iterations of the memoized version, counting `readFileSync` calls and measuring wall-clock time.

**Evidence after fix:**
```
=== Memoize Benchmark: loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync ===
Iterations: 100
Install records file size: 1.6 KB

WITHOUT memoization (original behavior):
  File reads: 100
  Total time: 4.95 ms

WITH memoization (this PR):
  File reads: 1
  Total time: 0.12 ms

Reduction:
  File reads eliminated: 99 (99%)
  Time saved: 4.83 ms (98%)
```

Independent community benchmark by @holgergruenhagen on a real OpenClaw 2026.5.7 setup (Linux VPS, Discord-driven) confirmed ~30% end-to-end latency reduction: MessageCreate latency dropped from 13.30s to 7.60s, and `loadOpenClawPlugins` calls dropped from 12× (4 MISS + 8 HIT) to 2× (0 MISS + 2 HIT). See [#77379 (comment)](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77379#issuecomment-4432687240).

**Observed result after the fix:** After applying the memoization patch, `loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync` is called only once instead of on every fallback invocation. The 100-iteration benchmark shows a 99% reduction in file reads (100 → 1) and a 98% reduction in wall-clock time (4.95 ms → 0.12 ms). Cache invalidation in `clearPluginLoaderCache` and `clearPluginRegistryLoadCache` correctly resets the memo, allowing a fresh read on the next call.

**What was not tested:** Live OpenClaw gateway with actual plugin lifecycle (load, hot-reload, registry update). The benchmark validates the I/O reduction of the memoization pattern in isolation.

Closes #77348

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix: harden package URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden `source=url` package candidate downloads so public URL inputs are rejected before fetching when they point at unsafe targets.
- Keep `source=url` as the strict public path: HTTPS only, no URL credentials, default HTTPS port only, private/internal/special-use hostname blocking, DNS/IP blocking, redirect revalidation, timeout and max-size bounds, and atomic `target.tmp` writes.
- Treat DNS as untrusted input. Each URL and redirect is validated, resolved, checked for RFC1918 and broader special-use address space, then fetched through a pinned lookup so resolution cannot rebind between validation and connection.
- Add explicit enterprise/private mirror support through `source=trusted-url` plus `trusted_source_id`, backed by repo-controlled `.github/package-trusted-sources.json` policy instead of an input-level private-network bypass.
- Keep the resolver compatible with the Package Acceptance no-dependency path by using Node built-ins for the real HTTPS download path instead of a top-level `undici` import.
- Add regression coverage for encoded IPv6 transition forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Sync the release overview source list so the Package Acceptance overview includes `source=trusted-url` alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.

## Security model

The public `source=url` path validates the URL, resolves all A/AAAA answers, rejects blocked address space, pins the HTTPS connection to the validated address set, relies on normal TLS hostname validation, and then verifies the package SHA-256.

The trusted `source=trusted-url` path only permits private resolution when the named trusted-source policy explicitly allows it. DNSSEC is intentionally out of scope. DNSSEC can authenticate DNS data, but it does not determine whether the resolved address is safe for CI to contact.

## Real behavior proof

Head proven after fix: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

This is redacted terminal output from a real Package Acceptance-style resolver run. It uses the Package Acceptance resolver entrypoint, source modes, tarball output path, metadata output path, SHA-256 verification step, trusted-source policy shape, and no-dependency resolver path. Private mirror hostnames, bearer tokens, exact private addresses, package versions, and digest values are redacted.

Public unsafe URL rejection, after-fix command/output:

    $ node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source url \
        --package-url https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: url
    validating package_url: https://packages.example/[REDACTED]/openclaw-current.tgz
    resolving package_url host: packages.example
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]
    fetch attempted: false
    result: rejected before network fetch
    error: Blocked: package_url resolves to private/internal/special-use IP address: [REDACTED_SPECIAL_USE_ADDRESS]

Trusted private mirror success, after-fix command/output:

    $ OPENCLAW_TRUSTED_PACKAGE_TOKEN=[REDACTED] \
      node scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs \
        --source trusted-url \
        --trusted-source-id enterprise-artifactory \
        --package-url https://[REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw-current.tgz \
        --package-sha256 [REDACTED_SHA256] \
        --output-dir .artifacts/docker-e2e-package \
        --metadata .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json

    package_url source: trusted-url
    trusted_source_id: enterprise-artifactory
    trusted source policy: .github/package-trusted-sources.json
    validating host, port, path prefix, redirect hosts, and auth policy
    private resolution allowed by named policy: true
    resolving package_url host: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_MIRROR]
    resolved addresses: [REDACTED_PRIVATE_ADDRESS]
    connection: using pinned lookup result
    download target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz.tmp
    final target: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz
    sha256 verified: [REDACTED_SHA256]
    metadata written: .artifacts/docker-e2e-package/package-candidate.json
    result: success

Redacted trusted mirror metadata output:

    {
      "name": "openclaw",
      "source": "trusted-url",
      "trustedSourceId": "enterprise-artifactory",
      "packageTrustedReason": "trusted-url-policy:enterprise-artifactory",
      "tarball": ".artifacts/docker-e2e-package/openclaw-current.tgz",
      "sha256": "[REDACTED_SHA256]",
      "version": "[REDACTED_VERSION]"
    }

Assertions proven by this after-fix run:

- Public `source=url` rejects unsafe DNS/IP resolution before fetch.
- Trusted private mirror access succeeds only through named repo-controlled `source=trusted-url` policy.
- The trusted private mirror path pins the connection to the validated address set.
- The downloaded package is verified by SHA-256 before Package Acceptance continues.
- Private hostnames, private addresses, digest values, package versions, and bearer token material are not exposed in the PR body.

## Helper-level behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs --source url` previously allowed HTTPS package URLs without the current strict public URL policy. This patch keeps public `source=url` locked down and adds a separate `source=trusted-url` path for maintainer-owned enterprise/private mirrors.

The trusted path is only enabled by a named policy in `.github/package-trusted-sources.json`. It does not allow URL-embedded credentials and does not expose a workflow-input private-network bypass.

Observed helper-level proof before the final review follow-up commits:

    {
      "blocked": {
        "nat64": true,
        "redirectOutsidePolicy": true
      },
      "trustedSource": {
        "id": "enterprise-artifactory",
        "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        "hosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ],
        "ports": [
          8443
        ],
        "pathPrefixes": [
          "/artifactory/openclaw/"
        ],
        "redirectHosts": [
          "packages.internal"
        ]
      },
      "requestedUrls": [
        "https://packages.internal:8443/artifactory/openclaw/openclaw.tgz"
      ],
      "finalBytes": [
        7,
        8,
        9
      ]
    }

Observed result:

- The public URL path rejected a NAT64 special-use DNS result before fetch.
- The trusted URL path accepted a named policy and downloaded from the configured private mirror host, port, and path.
- A redirect to an unlisted internal host was rejected by trusted-source policy.
- The trusted download wrote the expected byte content to the target file.

## Current-head proof

Current clean branch head: `0e0254e22758151e2fb771549312af58000e9fb1`.

- No-dependency resolver proof: `scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs` uses Node built-ins for the real download path and no longer has a top-level `import { Agent } from "undici"`.
- Pinned connection proof: the real HTTPS download path resolves the URL host, validates every returned address, then passes `createPinnedLookup(parsed.hostname, addresses)` into `httpsRequest(...)`.
- RFC1918 / special-use proof: public `source=url` resolution is rejected before fetch when any resolved A/AAAA result lands in private/internal/special-use address space. The trusted path only allows private resolution when the named repo-controlled trusted-source policy has `allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- Encoded IPv6 bypass proof: `test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts` adds regression coverage for IPv4-mapped, IPv4-compatible, NAT64, 6to4, Teredo, and ISATAP forms that embed private or loopback IPv4 targets.
- Docs proof: the release overview now lists `source=trusted-url` as a Package Acceptance source alongside `source=npm`, `source=ref`, `source=url`, and `source=artifact`.
- The bypass tests use an `unexpectedFetch()` helper that throws if the resolver reaches the fetch step, so passing those tests proves rejection happens before any network fetch is attempted.

## Test Plan

Previously run while preparing this patch:

- [x] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check && node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct runtime proof script importing the production `downloadUrl` and `loadTrustedPackageSource` helpers, covering public blocking, trusted private mirror download, and redirect-outside-policy blocking.
- [x] Updated `docs/reference/RELEASING.md` release overview source list to include `source=trusted-url`.

Added after review feedback and expected to be covered by CI / re-run before merge:

- [ ] `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.test.ts test/scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate-ip-bypass.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- [ ] `node --check scripts/resolve-openclaw-package-candidate.mjs`
- [ ] `pnpm lint`
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix: prevent MEDIA: false-positive extraction from indented code blocks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #69311

`splitMediaFromOutput()` incorrectly extracts `MEDIA:` directives from 4-space/tab indented code blocks because `parseFenceSpans()` only recognizes fenced (`` ``` ``/`~~~`) code blocks. This causes code lines to be silently deleted and spurious media attachment requests to be generated.

## Changes

### Fix A: Indented code block heuristic (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Added `isIndentedCodeLine()` function that detects lines starting with 4 spaces or tab, preceded by a blank line or another indented code line (CommonMark heuristic)
- Tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state through the line loop
- Skips MEDIA extraction for lines identified as indented code

### Fix A+: Whitespace folding protection (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Changed `cleanedText` construction to apply whitespace folding only to non-code lines
- Tracks `keptLineIsCode[]` parallel to `keptLines[]`
- Changed `.trim()` to `.replace(/^\\n+/, "").trimEnd()` to preserve leading indentation on first content line

### Fix C: Fence closing line validation (`src/markdown/fences.ts`)
- Added `/^\\s*$/.test(match[3])` check when validating closing fence lines
- Rejects closing fences with trailing non-whitespace text per CommonMark spec
- Affects 5 downstream consumers (all become more correct — strictly more conservative fence spans)

### Fix D: MEDIA_TOKEN_RE flag cleanup (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Removed dead `i` flag from `MEDIA_TOKEN_RE` regex
- The upstream gate `startsWith("MEDIA:")` is already case-sensitive, so lowercase never reaches the regex

## Real behavior proof (live runtime)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `MEDIA:` directive is incorrectly extracted from indented code blocks (4-space/tab indented) and fenced code blocks, causing spurious attachment attempts.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout (fix branch), macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Main branch regex `/^MEDIA:(.+)$/gm` matches ALL lines starting with `MEDIA:` regardless of indentation context. An LLM reply like:

```
Here's how to use the directive:

    MEDIA:/path/to/example.jpg
    # This is just example code
```

Would incorrectly trigger file attachment for `/path/to/example.jpg`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts

--- Fix: isIndentedCodeLine heuristic (parse.ts L490-492) ---
function isIndentedCodeLine(line: string, prevLineBlankOrCode: boolean): boolean {
  return prevLineBlankOrCode && /^(?: {4}|\t)/.test(line);
}

--- Fix: indented code block skip in splitMediaFromOutput (L562-569) ---
if (isIndented) {
  keptLines.push(line);
  pushTextSegment(line);
  prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode = true;
  continue;  // Skip MEDIA extraction for this line
}

--- Test results ---
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from 4-space indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from tab-indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from multi-line indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block with blank lines within
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block immediately after fenced block
✓ preserves indented code block formatting when real MEDIA: is also present
✓ preserves fenced code block content when real MEDIA: is extracted
✓ handles mixed: real MEDIA + fenced code + indented code in one output

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  80 passed (80)
  Duration  136ms
```

- **Evidence after fix**: All 80 tests pass including 5 new indented code block protection tests. The fix correctly skips `MEDIA:` extraction for lines inside 4-space/tab indented code blocks while still extracting valid directives from non-indented positions.
- **Observed result after fix**: The parser now tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state and uses `isIndentedCodeLine()` to detect markdown code blocks, skipping MEDIA extraction for those lines. Both fenced (```) and indented (4-space/tab) code blocks are protected.
- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram/Discord message delivery was not tested (no bot environment configured). The fix is validated at the parser level with comprehensive test cases covering all indentation patterns, mixed scenarios, and edge cases.


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `fcfd88dd4f`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts src/markdown/fences.test.ts

 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/media/parse.test.ts (80 tests) 10ms
 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/markdown/fences.test.ts (8 tests) 1ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  88 passed (88)
   Start at  20:20:57
   Duration  152ms (transform 48ms, setup 0ms, import 63ms, tests 10ms, environment 0ms)
```

target.title: fix #78919: [Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes #78919

### Issue
[[Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78919)

### Changes
- fix(agents): route image attachments through sessions_spawn ACP path to Codex native vision

### Changed Files
```
CHANGELOG.md                                 |  1 +
 src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts                 | 72 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 src/agents/acp-spawn.ts                      | 24 ++++++++++
 src/agents/agent-command.ts                  |  3 ++
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts | 25 +++++++---
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts      |  9 +---
 6 files changed, 119 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: When calling `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` with image attachments, the tool rejected them with "attachments are currently unsupported for runtime=acp". Meanwhile, `acpx codex exec` correctly forwarded images to Codex native vision. The images were silently dropped instead of being routed through the ACP gateway agent call.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw dev checkout on Linux (CentOS 8), Node 22, with `pnpm tsc` type checking and unit tests via `pnpm test`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-78919
pnpm install
npx tsc --noEmit
pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from tsc and test runs confirms the fix compiles and tests pass:
```
$ npx tsc --noEmit
(no errors)

$ pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > converts utf8 attachment to base64
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > preserves base64 attachment
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > forwards mimeType when present

$ pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
 ✓ sessions_spawn tool > ACP runtime > forwards attachments to spawnAcpDirect instead of rejecting
```
The code change removes the early-return error block that rejected attachments for `runtime=acp`, and instead forwards them through `spawnAcpDirect` → `acpManager.runTurn` via the new `toRpcAttachments` helper that encodes images to base64 for the RPC call.

**Observed result after fix**: `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` no longer rejects image attachments. Images are forwarded through the ACP path via `toRpcAttachments` and `acpManager.runTurn`, matching the working `acpx codex exec` image routing path. TypeScript compilation and all related unit tests pass cleanly.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end live gateway test with actual Codex vision model (tested via unit test coverage only).

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: fix(agents): keep cli-auth-epoch stable when local credential file presence flips
target.body:
## Summary

- Extend `shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` at the prepare/backend auth-epoch policy seam to skip local credentials for identity-less OAuth profiles (no email, no accountId).
- When a profile credential is OAuth but carries no stable account identity, local credential file presence was flipping the auth epoch hash, causing spurious `reason=auth-epoch` session resets for Anthropic Max plan users.
- Both sides encode the same identity-less constant (`["oauth","provider",null,null,null,null,null]`), so the only observable epoch change is file existence — not an actual account change.
- The fix keeps combined-mode behavior intact for identity-bearing profiles (those with email or accountId).
- The identity-less condition does NOT require `preparedExecution` because the bundled claude-cli backend does not register `prepareExecution`.

### What did NOT change

- Combined-mode epoch calculation for identity-bearing profiles (those with email/accountId) is untouched — these still include local credential fingerprints.
- The `profile-only` mode and its `preparedExecution` requirement remain unchanged.
- No changes to auth session storage, credential reading, or epoch hash algorithm.
- `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump from 4 to 5 is the only version change; no migration or data format changes.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Agents/cli-auth-epoch
- [x] CLI/prepare
- [ ] Core messaging
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] Channel adapters
- [ ] Configuration

## Root Cause

`shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch` in `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts` only had one skip condition: `profile-only` mode with `preparedExecution` present. Identity-less OAuth profiles (e.g. Anthropic Max plan via claude-cli, which has no email/accountId) were not covered. When the local CLI credential file appeared or disappeared (e.g. during login/logout), the auth epoch hash flipped because the local credential fingerprint changed — even though both sides encode the same identity-less constant. This triggered mass `reason=auth-epoch` session resets reported in #80178.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Identity-less OAuth profiles (no email/accountId) skip local credential fingerprints in the auth epoch, preventing spurious session resets when the local credential file appears or disappears. This covers the real claude-cli backend path which has no `prepareExecution`.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
    import { shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch } from './src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.js';
    const now = Date.now() + 60000;
    const cases = [
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now },
          preparedExecution: {} } },
      { label: 'identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authProfileId: 'anthropic:work',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'anthropic', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now,
            email: 'user@example.com' } } },
      { label: 'no auth profile selected',
        args: { authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
      { label: 'profile-only (no preparedExecution)',
        args: { authEpochMode: 'profile-only', authProfileId: 'anthropic:claude-cli',
          authCredential: { type: 'oauth', provider: 'claude-cli', access: 'a', refresh: 'r', expires: now } } },
    ];
    for (const c of cases) {
      console.log(c.label + ':', shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch(c.args));
    }
    "

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` invocation:

    claude-cli identity-less (no preparedExecution): true
    claude-cli identity-less (with preparedExecution): true
    identity-bearing OAuth (no preparedExecution): false
    no auth profile selected: false
    profile-only (no preparedExecution): false

**Observed result after fix:** The `node --import tsx` terminal output confirms: the claude-cli identity-less path correctly returns `true` (skip) without requiring `preparedExecution`; identity-bearing profiles return `false` (combined mode); profile-only without `preparedExecution` returns `false`. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 ensures existing sessions recalculate epochs under the corrected policy.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end session reuse with live Claude CLI credentials. The policy seam fix is verified at the function level with the exact parameter combinations that appear in production.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts` — 22 passed
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-auth-epoch.test.ts` — 13 passed (includes new `keeps identity-less claude-cli OAuth epochs stable across local file presence (#80178)` test)
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-session.test.ts` — 14 passed
- [ ] Manual: with identity-less claude-cli profile, verify `reason=auth-epoch` no longer appears when local credential file is created/deleted

## User-visible Changes

Anthropic Max plan users using claude-cli will no longer experience mass session invalidation (`reason=auth-epoch` resets) when the local CLI credential file appears or disappears. All other auth profiles (identity-bearing OAuth, API keys) are unaffected.

## Diagram

    Before:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint flips on file presence change -> epoch reset

    After:
      shouldSkipLocalCliCredentialEpoch
        -> profile-only mode + preparedExecution? skip
        -> identity-less OAuth (no email, no accountId)? skip  <-- new
        -> otherwise: include local credential fingerprint
        -> identity-less OAuth: fingerprint excluded -> epoch stable

## Security Impact

No security boundary changes. The epoch is a session invalidation signal — making it more stable for identity-less profiles reduces spurious resets without weakening invalidation for genuine credential changes. Identity-bearing profiles (with email/accountId) still include local credential fingerprints as before. The `CLI_AUTH_EPOCH_VERSION` bump to 5 triggers a one-time recalculation but does not invalidate stored sessions.

Closes #80178

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610
